evals.prompt: We have a 4,000-test pytest regression suite and suspect a lot of overlap. How do I find out which lines and branches each test uniquely covers, so I know which tests are pulling their weight?
expected_patterns: --cov-context=test OR cov-context
coverage\.py OR coverage.py OR pytest-cov
switch_context OR dynamic context OR per-test context
branch OR --cov-branch OR --branch
unique OR uniquely covered OR only test covering
sqlite OR \.coverage OR coverage database OR contexts table
anti_patterns: single combined coverage report
--cov=. with no context
line count per test file as the metric
delete tests with low overall coverage percentage

evals.prompt: Test A and Test B both cover exactly the same set of lines according to our per-test coverage data. Can I just delete one? Walk me through whether identical coverage actually proves redundancy.
expected_patterns: coverage equality does not prove (equivalent OR same assertions OR redundant)
mutation OR mutant OR PIT OR Stryker OR fault detection
different assertions OR different inputs OR different oracle
covers same lines but (asserts OR checks) different
keep OR retain OR do not delete (when|if) assertions differ
anti_patterns: identical coverage means safe to delete
delete the duplicate immediately
coverage is the only signal needed
same lines covered therefore same test

evals.prompt: Cluster our test suite to surface near-duplicate tests that were probably copy-pasted. What's a defensible technique that won't just flag every test in the same file?
expected_patterns: AST OR abstract syntax tree
similarity OR jaccard OR cosine OR edit distance OR token similarity
cluster OR clustering OR hierarchical OR agglomerative
threshold OR similarity threshold OR cutoff
coverage (profile|signature|fingerprint) OR execution profile
review OR flag for review OR human (review|confirm)
anti_patterns: group tests by filename
exact string match only
delete all tests in a cluster automatically
use raw line numbers as similarity

evals.prompt: Mine our last 12 months of CI history. I want to find tests that have literally never failed and tests that are always flaky. Then tell me what each finding means for the suite.
expected_patterns: pass rate OR fail rate OR flip rate OR transition
never failed OR zero failures OR 100% pass
flaky OR same SHA OR same commit (pass and fail|different results)
junit OR test result history OR CI history OR Datadog OR Trunk
never-failing does not mean (delete|useless) OR low (defect-detection|signal) value
quarantine OR investigate OR fix the flake (before|not) delete
anti_patterns: delete every test that never failed
a green test is a useless test
flaky tests should be deleted to clean up CI
single run determines flakiness

evals.prompt: Give me the decision rules for what to do with each test we audit. I keep conflating 'redundant', 'obsolete', and 'low-value' and just deleting things.
expected_patterns: redundant.*(another test|covers the same|subsumed)
obsolete.*(feature removed|dead code|no longer)
low.?value.*(never failed|trivial|getter|no assertion)
keep.*(unique coverage|catches defects|risk)
different (action|disposition) per (category|class)
redundant => (delete|merge) OR obsolete => delete OR low-value => (quarantine|review)
anti_patterns: treat all three categories the same
delete anything that looks redundant
low value means delete immediately
one rule for all flagged tests

evals.prompt: Restructure our flat 3,000-test suite into smoke, core, and extended tiers. What evidence decides which tier a test lands in?
expected_patterns: smoke.*(fast|critical|minutes|few)
extended OR full OR nightly OR slow
risk OR business-critical OR critical path
defect.?detection history OR tests that (caught|found) (real )?bugs OR fault-finding
(runtime|duration|execution time) as a (tier|tiering) input
tag OR marker OR pytest mark OR @pytest.mark OR label
anti_patterns: tier by test file alphabetically
smoke = first N tests
random sample for smoke
tier purely by how fast tests run

evals.prompt: Before we delete 600 tests flagged as redundant, what's the safe rollout? I do not want to nuke them in one PR and find out three weeks later we lost real coverage.
expected_patterns: quarantine OR grace period OR soft-delete OR skip (before|not) delete
@pytest.mark.skip OR xfail OR exclude OR move to (quarantine|deprecated) (dir|suite)
human (sign-?off|approval|review) OR code owner OR CODEOWNERS
(2|two|several) (week|sprint|release) OR grace window OR observation period
(restore|revert|recover) OR git history OR keep the commit
monitor OR watch for (escaped|missed) (defects|bugs|regressions)
anti_patterns: delete all 600 in one PR
rm the test files
no review needed for redundant tests
deletion is reversible so just do it

evals.prompt: Produce the 'what we deleted and why' record for an audit so a future engineer (or auditor) can defend every removal. What goes in it?
expected_patterns: per-test OR per-deletion OR one (row|entry) per test
reason OR justification OR category (redundant|obsolete|low-value)
(subsumed by|covered by|replaced by) (which|the) test OR superseding test id
coverage (delta|impact|before/after) OR lines no longer covered
(date|commit|PR|sha) OR approver OR signed off by
restore OR how to (recover|revert) OR quarantine location
anti_patterns: just a count of deleted tests
no per-test justification
trust me it was redundant
no record of who approved

evals.prompt: A test we want to cut has never failed in 18 months of CI AND is fully covered (same lines) by another test. Is that enough to delete it? Reason through the gotcha.
expected_patterns: not (sufficient|enough) on its own OR still verify OR not automatically
(mutation|mutant) OR does the OTHER test (assert|catch|kill) what this one does
never failed (because|may mean) (low (risk|change)|stable code) not (worthless|redundant)
different (assertion|input|edge case|oracle) could still be unique
quarantine OR observe OR grace period (before|then) delete
anti_patterns: two green signals so delete it
never-failed plus same-coverage equals safe delete
coverage overlap is conclusive
no further checks required
